title: About Me
text:
I am an Environmental Studies student at the University of California, Santa Barbara with interests in GIS, data science, and environmental policy.
My academic work focuses on how spatial data and mapping tools can reveal patterns in environmental systems and highlight inequalities in the way environmental resources are distributed.
Some of the areas I am especially interested in include:

Geographic Information Systems (GIS)
Data analysis and spatial visualization
Environmental justice
Resource distribution and environmental inequities

In the future, I hope to pursue a career in law and politics, where I can work on environmental policy and address issues related to environmental justice and equitable access to environmental resources.

title: About Me
text:
Heritage and Culture
My cultural background is shaped by both my Filipino and Italian heritage, which have each played an important role in my upbringing.
My Filipino grandmother lived with my family for most of my life, which helped me stay closely connected to Filipino culture while growing up. Through her, we maintained strong ties with our local Filipino community and participated in traditions that emphasize family, respect, and community connection.
On my Italian side, culture is often expressed through food, hospitality, and gathering together. Sharing meals and welcoming others into the home are traditions that have always been central to family life.
Together, these cultural influences have shaped my appreciation for community, tradition, and the importance of bringing people together.

title: About Me
text:
Personal Interests
Outside of academics, I enjoy activities that help me stay active, creative, and engaged with others.
I enjoy hiking because it gives me the chance to spend time outdoors and connect with the kinds of landscapes I study in my coursework. I also enjoy painting, which gives me a creative outlet and a way to experiment with color and design.
Another important part of my life has been coaching and teaching through the NCOS program. Working with younger students has been a rewarding experience because it allows me to support others, build confidence, and strengthen my own leadership and communication skills.

title: Home
text: I’m and Environmental Studies Student at UCSB interested in GIS, Environmental Justice and politics, and how we can utilize spacial tools to understand real-world issues. This website acts as a personal online portfolio where I can share some of the projects I’ve worked on, along with information about my interest, experiences, and the skills I’ve been developing. Through these pages, I hope to highlight ways I use mapping, data, and environmental analysis to explore and communicate interesting environmental topics.

title: Soil Permeability Around Landfills
text:
This GIS project analyzes soil permeability surrounding the Calabasas and Chiquita Canyon landfill sites in Los Angeles County.
Using USDA SSURGO soil data, soil classes were grouped based on infiltration potential to better understand how subsurface conditions may influence water movement and contaminant transport.
This project was completed using ArcGIS Pro and focuses on simplifying soil classifications to make environmental patterns easier to interpret.

title: Soil Permeability Around Landfills
text:
Why this project matters
This project helped me think more critically about how soil conditions can influence environmental risk. It also showed me how GIS can be used to simplify complex environmental datasets into maps that are easier to interpret and communicate.



Description
Based on their soil series descriptions in the USDA SSURGO database, soil map units were grouped by permeability to highlight differences in hydrologic behavior surrounding the Calabasas and Chiquita Canyon landfill sites. Coarser Hanford and Yolo soils exhibit higher permeability and allow greater infiltration, while clay-rich Altamont and Diablo soils have lower permeability and may slow contaminant movement. River and mountainous units were separated as non-soil landscape features.


Soil Stability at the Calabasas Landfill
Although the Calabasas Landfill is located in the Santa Monica Mountains which is a non-soil feature, it can potentially be concerning because the area is defined by hillside terrain and soil conditions that can be vulnerable to erosion and slope instability. Disturbing these soils can increase the chances of sediment movement, and harmful runoff especially during heavy rain events. This means the main risk at Calabasas is less about high permeability and more about the potential for unstable slopes and surface erosion to spread pollutants beyond the landfill site.


Soil Stability at the Chiquita Canyon Landfill
The Chiquita Canyon area appears especially concerning because it is near highly permeable soil. This increases the risk that contaminants could easily infiltrate the soil and then be carried through runoff into the nearby Santa Clara River Watershed. Because that river ultimately drains toward the ocean, pollution released near the landfill could travel beyond the immediate site and potentially affect downstream ecosystems overall.


Summary:
Both landfill sites raise soil-related environmental concerns, but in different ways.
Chiquita Canyon is concerning as it’s near highly permeable soils, which can potential contaminants to be absorbed into the local watershed and move quickly into the nearby Santa Clara River that supplies over 60,000 acres of local farmland (ASHS, 2013).
Calabasas, on the other hand, is more concerning for erosion and slope instability, where disturbed hillside soils can increase runoff and the downslope spread of pollutants. Together, these sites show how local soil conditions can shape the type and scale of environmental risk around landfills.

title: Personal Data Coding Project
text: This project comes from my personal data assignment, where I worked with a data set I collected myself and used R to explore patterns through visualization. I wanted to understand how different daily factors relate to my reported energy level, and then think about how those patterns could be translated into a more expressive affective visualization.

title: Personal Data Coding Project
text:
Step 1. Set Up

library(tidyverse) #load in tidyverse package
library(here) #load in here
library(janitor) #load in janitor 

personal <- read_csv(here("data", "ENVSdsresearch.csv")) |> #create new data set called personal using my ENVSdsresearch.csv 
  clean_names() |> #clean names 
  mutate(date = as.Date(date, format = "%m/%d/%Y")) #change date format in table 

Here I loaded in the packages that I used to create my project. This includes, tidyverse, here, and janitor. I then read in my ENVS research data into a new data frame called “personal.”
Under this, I cleaned and wrangled the data names and formatted the date to be xm/xd/xy.

title: Personal Data Coding Project
text:
Step 2. Creating necessary data frame

latest_date <- max(personal$date, na.rm = TRUE) # create new data frame called latest_date that establishes the last date that data was collected

Although this step seems small, it is important to create the data frames we will recall in the upcoming code chunks.

title: Personal Data Coding Project
text:
Step 3. Creating Visualization with Code

ggplot(personal, aes(x = cloud_cover, y = energy_level_1_5, color = cloud_cover)) + #load in data and assign x, y varaibles.
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.25, outlier.shape = NA) + #shaping geom boxplot
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) + #sizing for jitter plot
  labs( 
    title = "Daily Energy Level by Cloud Cover", #title
    subtitle = paste("Most recent observation:", latest_date), #subtitle with latest date of recorded observations
    x = "Cloud cover",
    y = "Energy Level (1–5)" #label x and y axis 
  ) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + #color palate and theme selections
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








This figure shows how my reported daily energy level changed across different cloud cover conditions in my personal dataset. The x-axis groups days by cloud cover category, while the y-axis shows energy level on a 1–5 scale.
Each colored point represents an individual observation. The boxplots summarize the distribution within each category by showing the median, the middle 50% of values, and the overall spread of the data. From this plot, it looks like energy levels were generally highest on mostly clear days, where the median appears around 4 and the values are spread across a relatively wide range. Cloudy days also show moderate to somewhat high energy levels, with a median around the mid-3 range. In contrast, cloudy/rainy days appear to be associated with somewhat lower energy levels overall, and partly cloudy has very limited data, so it is harder to interpret confidently.
Overall, the plot suggests that clearer weather may have been associated with higher self-reported energy in this dataset, although the number of observations differs across categories, so the results should be interpreted cautiously.

title: StoryMap: Environmental Impacts of Southern California Targeted Landfills
text:
Solid waste management and its associated infrastructure is one of the most spatially consequential yet under-examined aspects of urban infrastructure. In Los Angeles County alone, millions of tons of waste is generated each year and the landfills that hold it don’t exist in a vacuum — they sit within real landscapes, near real communities, and on soils that connect to real watersheds. Yet landfills are often treated as out-of-sight, out-of-mind infrastructure, even as the communities nearest to them bear disproportionate environmental and health burdens.
Examining where landfills are located, what surrounds them, and who lives nearby reveals patterns that matter for environmental justice, public health, and land-use planning. Soil permeability determines how quickly contaminants can reach groundwater. Topography shapes whether pollutants stay contained or spread. And income levels often predict which communities end up adjacent to facilities that wealthier neighborhoods have successfully kept out.
This study uses GIS analysis to move beyond the abstract and examine two real facilities — the Calabasas Landfill and the Chiquita Canyon Landfill — to understand how place shapes risk.

title: StoryMap: Environmental Impacts of Southern California Targeted Landfills
text:
View the StoryMap
Open the StoryMap

title: StoryMap: Environmental Impacts of Southern California Targeted Landfills
text: StoryMap Preview

title: StoryMap: Environmental Impacts of Southern California Targeted Landfills
text:
Why this project matters
This comparative analysis of the Calabasas and Chiquita Canyon Landfills reveals that while both facilities serve the same fundamental purpose, their environmental and social footprints differ substantially — shaped by the landscapes they occupy and the communities that surround them.
On soil stability, Chiquita Canyon’s proximity to highly permeable soils poses a serious groundwater contamination risk, particularly given its documented leachate issues and the nearby Santa Clara River Watershed. Calabasas, by contrast, presents greater concerns around erosion and slope instability given its hillside terrain in the Santa Monica Mountains, where disturbed soils can mobilize pollutants during rain events. Neither site is without significant soil-related risk — they simply manifest differently based on local geology.
The socioeconomic data shows more contrast. Income per capita hotspot analysis shows that Calabasas is surrounded by high-income communities like Hidden Hills and Agoura Hills, while Chiquita Canyon sits amid significantly lower-income areas, a pattern consistent with decades of environmental justice research showing that locally undesirable land uses are disproportionately sited in lower-income communities. The cancer incidence analysis adds another dimension: elevated rates appear closer to Chiquita Canyon (within 5,000m) compared to a broader radius around Calabasas (within 10,000m), though the data cannot establish direct causation without cancer-type-specific analysis.
Together, these findings suggest that the Chiquita Canyon community faces compounding disadvantages — greater contamination risk, lower socioeconomic resources to respond, and elevated cancer incidence in closer proximity to the facility. Future research should examine specific cancer types, conduct longitudinal groundwater monitoring, and assess cumulative environmental burden to better understand the full scope of these landfills’ impacts on Southern California communities.
